comments.text: Should this problem be restated to clarify that it's for a, b, c >= 2 ? It seems there is an obvious counterexample for the a, b, c >=1 case, a = 1, b = 5, c = 7. Edit: Looking at the lean formalization, this does appear to be the case. The >= in the problem statement is a typo, it should be > instead.
You are talking about the case of two powers, which is already tackled in problems #845 and #1110
If $a,b,c$ are allowed to take the value $1$, then the answer to the problem question as stated is trivially no. Just choose $a=b=c=1$.

The following two papers contain the most recent results on this problem, as far as I know. M. M. Ma, Y. G. Chen, On d-complete sequences of integers, Journal of Number Theory, Volume 164, 1–12, 2016. Y. G. Chen, W. X. Yu, On d-complete sequences of integers, II, Acta Arithmetica, Volume 207, 861–181, 2023. For example, with $a = 2$ and $b = 5$, this is now known for all $1 < c \le 87$ with $\gcd(c, 10) = 1$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
